documents.title: 🧬 DP Thinking

documents.title: 🚀 Optimization Thinking

documents.title: 🎚️ State Thinking
documents.summary: Many problems reduce to: "What's the minimal information needed to determine future behavior?" That's the state.

documents.title: 🏗️ Systems Thinking
documents.summary: In real engineering, the bottleneck is rarely a single algorithm — it's how components interact. Systems thinking asks: where is the constraint? what's the blast radius? what fails first?

documents.title: 🎲 Probabilistic Thinking

documents.title: 🏢 Service Companies Track

documents.title: 💹 Fintech & Quant/Trading Track

documents.title: 🔧 Core & Hardware / Systems Track
documents.summary: For those who love low-level systems, C/C++, hardware, and embedded. Different emphasis: systems knowledge + C + domain over [LeetCode](https://leetcode.com) grinding.

documents.title: 🚀 Startups & Early-Stage Track
documents.summary: A DIFFERENT game: less DSA puzzle-grinding, more "can you BUILD and SHIP?" Your projects, practical skills, and versatility matter most.

documents.title: 🎯 Off-Campus & Referrals Track

documents.title: 💎 SOLVED PROBLEMS VAULT — Full Editorials

documents.summary: This is the missing half of the Codex. Everywhere else teaches concepts and points you to problems. **Here, every problem is a complete editorial.**

documents.title: Contains Duplicate

documents.summary: Given an array of strings `strs`, **group the anagrams** together. You can return the answer in any order.

documents.title: Top K Frequent Elements
documents.summary: Given an integer array `nums` and an integer `k`, return the `k` **most frequent** elements. You may return the answer in any order.

documents.summary: Given an integer array `nums`, return an array `answer` such that `answer[i]` is equal to the **product of all the elements of `nums` except `nums[i]`**.

documents.title: Longest Consecutive Sequence

documents.summary: Given an unsorted array of integers `nums`, return the **length of the longest consecutive elements sequence**.

documents.title: Two Sum II – Input Array Is Sorted
documents.summary: Given a **1-indexed** array of integers `numbers` that is already **sorted in non-decreasing order**, find two numbers such that they add up to a specific `target` number. Return the indices of the two numbers (1-indexed) as `[index1, index2]` where `index1 < index2`.

documents.title: 3Sum

documents.title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
documents.summary: Given a string `s`, find the length of the **longest substring** without repeating characters.

documents.title: Longest Repeating Character Replacement
documents.summary: You are given a string `s` and an integer `k`. You can choose any character and change it to any other uppercase English letter. You can perform this operation at most `k` times.

documents.title: Permutation in String
documents.summary: Given two strings `s1` and `s2`, return `true` if `s2` contains a permutation of `s1`, or `false` otherwise. (i.e., one of `s1`'s permutations is a substring of `s2`.)

documents.title: Minimum Window Substring
documents.summary: Given two strings `s` and `t`, return the minimum window substring of `s` such that every character in `t` (including duplicates) is included in the window. If no such window exists, return `""`.

documents.title: Sliding Window Maximum
documents.summary: You are given an array of integers `nums` and a sliding window of size `k` moving from left to right. Return the max value in each window position.

documents.title: Min Stack

documents.title: Evaluate Reverse Polish Notation

documents.title: Daily Temperatures

If there is no future day for which this is possible, keep `answer[i] == 0`.

This chapter teaches you to write HTML the way professionals do: **semantic, accessible, and well-structured**, not a soup of `<div>`s.

With these, you can build any layout deliberately.

documents.title: ⚡ JavaScript Mastery

documents.title: 🎯 The `this` Keyword & Prototypes

documents.title: 🚀 ES6+ Modern Features

This file is your tour of the modern syntax you'll see in every codebase: modules, template literals, optional chaining, nullish coalescing, `Map`/`Set`, generators, and iterators.

documents.title: 🔷 TypeScript
documents.summary: TypeScript is JavaScript with a **static type system** bolted on top. You write types; the TypeScript compiler checks them and then erases them, emitting plain JavaScript that runs anywhere. The payoff: bugs caught before you run the code, fearless refactoring, and an editor that actually understands your program.

The patterns here build directly on generics (file 03) — React's hooks are generic functions.

The **Java Collections Framework** (in `java.util`) is a set of interfaces and implementations that cover all of these, with consistent APIs.

documents.summary: Most tools store changes as *deltas* (file A changed these lines). Git is different: **every commit is a full snapshot** of your project, with unchanged files stored as pointers to identical previous content. Internally it's a content-addressed key-value store — Git hashes content with SHA-1/SHA-256 and that hash *is* the address.

documents.summary: Before the grammars and the garbage collectors, the mindset. This section is the *why*: why programming languages exist at all, why there are thousands of them, why you should learn to think across them, and how to read every language as a set of answers to the same eternal questions.

documents.summary: A CPU understands exactly one thing: **binary-encoded instructions**. A stream of bytes like `48 89 E5` that means, to an x86-64 processor, "move the stack pointer into the base pointer." Nothing else. No variables, no functions, no `if`, no strings — those are all *human fictions* we layer on top.

documents.summary: Concurrency enables parallelism but isn't the same thing. A web server handling 10,000 connections is *concurrent* (10,000 logical tasks) and may be *parallel* (running on 8 cores). You can have concurrency without parallelism (async on one thread) and parallelism without much concurrency (a parallel matrix multiply).

documents.title: 🛡️ Type Systems — The Science of Preventing Bad Programs
documents.summary: A **type** classifies values by what they are and what you can do with them. A **type system** is the set of rules a language uses to assign types and reject programs that misuse them. It is the single most powerful tool for catching bugs *before* the program ever runs — a lightweight, automatic proof system built into your compiler.

This section takes you to the metal.

documents.title: ♻️ Garbage Collection
topics: IV. THE KEY INSIGHT — GENERATIONAL GC
documents.summary: Manual memory management (`malloc`/`free`) is error-prone: forget to free → leak; free too early → use-after-free; free twice → corruption. **Garbage collection (GC)** automates deallocation: the runtime figures out which heap objects are no longer reachable and reclaims them, so the programmer never calls `free`.
